figures.caption: Table 2: Behavioral fidelity and single-request profile on the RTX 3060, full libero object suite (200 episodes/arch). step: wall-clock per environment step (amortized over chunk replay); inf : server-side forward; na: chunk length; brackets are 95% Wilson intervals.

Table 3: Per-step latency (ms) across the three tiers and resident footprint on the 8 GB Orin Nano. †Simulator offloaded to a second machine (co-resident server, client, and simulator exceed 8 GB), so latency includes a network hop. ∗Does not fit: weights exhaust the 8 GB pool.

Figure 2: Roofline placement of single-request VLA inference (LIBERO-Object sweep) on the RTX 3060 (sm 86; 25.6 TFLOP/s FP16, 360 GB/s) and Jetson AGX Orin (sm 87; 21.3 TFLOP/s, 204.8 GB/s). Each dot is one model at its operational intensity (FLOPs/byte) versus achieved throughput (estimated FLOPs over measured server-side time per call); the label is the achieved fraction of device peak, and the gap to the roof is the remaining utilization headroom.
Table 1: Why common serving stacks are complementary, not competing, for the VLA inference pattern on edge. ✓ supported; × not supported; ◦ partial or per-model, per-device engineering.

Figure 1: Architecture of vla.cpp. A stateless C++ server loads one GGUF bundle, runs the VLA inference pattern on each Protobuf observation, and returns a denormalized action chunk over ZeroMQ; a thin Python client owns closed-loop control and drives a simulator or robot.

Figure 3: Replan-interval study for SmolVLA on libero object (Jetson Orin Nano, 50 episodes/setting). Task success (left axis, 95% Wilson intervals) is stable across the shaded plateau and collapses once execution becomes fully open-loop at S=50. The amortized control rate (right axis) rises almost linearly with S.

Table 12: Replan-interval study for SmolVLA on libero object (Jetson Orin Nano, 50 episodes/setting). S is the executed steps per inference call; latency is per call, the control rate is amortized over the executed steps, and success carries a 95% Wilson interval.

Table 5: The seven served architectures. FM = flow-matching; AltVL = AlternateVL. T is the number of solver steps the iterative action head integrates per action chunk. The single-pass BitVLA head has no solver loop.
